cataloging_subject.label: The Lay of Havelok the Dane
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English verse romance conventionally titled The Lay of Havelok the Dane. The cataloging subject is the medieval work represented by the substantially complete text in Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108 and by collateral portions on three of the four physical scraps in Cambridge University Library MS Add. 4407 (19). It is not the whole Laud codex, either physical witness, the related Anglo-Norman and chronicle traditions, Frederic Madden's 1828 edition, Walter W. Skeat's 1868 re-edition or 1911 fragment transcription, the University of Michigan encoding, its page images, or the checked-in repository XML.

catalog_summary.title: The Lay of Havelok the Dane
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Middle English poet
catalog_summary.editor: Walter W. Skeat, re-editing Frederic Madden's 1828 text and directly checking Laud Misc. 108
catalog_summary.date: End of the thirteenth century; precise dating remains disputed
catalog_summary.region: England; Northeast Midlands composition localization, distinct from west-Norfolk language in the Laud witness

titles.value: The Lay of Havelok the Dane

assertion.notes: This is a conventional editorial title, not an authorial title preserved at the opening of the poem.

titles.value: Incipit vita Hauelok, quondam Rex Anglie et Denemarchie
titles.supplied_by: Rubric in Laud Misc. 108, partly cropped at the opening

scope.description: Opening rubric in the substantially complete witness

assertion.notes: The Latin rubric belongs to the witness presentation and does not make the Middle English poem a Latin work or establish a hagiographic genre by itself.

titles.value: Herknet to me, gode men, / Wiues, maydnes, and alle men

scope.description: Opening two lines in the substantially complete witness tradition

titles.value: Þat ihesu crist his soule bringe / Bi-forn his fader at his endinge

scope.description: First edition of the English poem in 1828, directly transcribed from Laud Misc. 108
1868 re-edition, directly checked against Laud Misc. 108 and incorporating Madden's corrected prior edition

notes: Skeat read the proof sheets against the manuscript twice; Madden also supplied corrections from his own re-comparison and reviewed the revises.

date_statements.display: End of the thirteenth century; precise date disputed

assertion.notes: The Database of Middle English Romance gives the end of the thirteenth century. Madden proposed 1270–1290; the modern dating survey records influential arguments extending the possible composition window into the opening decade of the fourteenth century. No exact year is normalized.

date_statements.display: Late thirteenth or early fourteenth century in Laud Misc. 108

scope.description: Date bounds of the containing codex; the Havelok/King Horn hand is more narrowly described as late thirteenth or early fourteenth century

assertion.notes: The normalized 1290–1325 interval maps the catalog's not-before 1290 and its late-thirteenth- or early-fourteenth-century Hand C description. The containing codex's separately recorded broader 1290–1400 bounds are not used as the work's attestation interval. This is an attestation date, not a composition date.

scope.description: First printing of the rediscovered Middle English poem

assertion.notes: This specialist-database localization describes the proposed place of composition, not the origin of either surviving physical witness.

scope.description: Scribal dialect of hand C in Laud Misc. 108

assertion.notes: The Bodleian record links the Havelok hand to LAEME entry 3 and west Norfolk. This localizes witness language, not the poet.

form_statements.description: A narrative poem in short rhyming couplets, described by the Database of Middle English Romance as aa4 bb4 and by DIMEV as two-line couplets. The 97 LG containers in the U-M XML reproduce Skeat's editorial paragraph/display spacing and are not evidence for 97 intrinsic stanzas.

assertion.notes: Bell and Couch describe the established Matter of England classification while emphasizing that Laud's hagiographical context complicates a purely secular reading.
The poem frames legendary dynastic restoration against English and Danish history; the label does not assert that its events are historical fact.
Religious invocations, providential signs, and Laud's vita rubric remain important, but the primary modern classification is romance rather than a saint's life.

subject_statements.label: Disguise, prophetic dreams, and tokens of recognition
Labor, merchant disguise, and social mobility

content_parts.label: Skeat/U-M representation of the substantially complete Laud text
content_parts.summary: The poem as printed by Skeat in 1868 and encoded by U-M: conventional lines 1–3001, with lines 1445–1624 absent after the loss of one Laud leaf. The encoded direct poem stream has 2,821 extant verbal lines; it does not incorporate the eleven additional verse lines first published from the Cambridge fragments in 1911.

locators.value: BODY/DIV1[@TYPE='poem']; 2,823 direct LG/L positions, of which 2 are asterisk-only lacuna rows and 2,821 are extant verbal verse lines
AHA2626.0001.001; printed pp. 1–86; conventional lines 1–3001 with lines 1445–1624 absent
ff. 204ra–219va; missing quire-19 leaf after f. 211 causes loss of conventional lines 1445–1624; Havelok ends before King Horn begins in the same f. 219va column
canvases 423–454 of 496 / bundle images/000423.jpg–images/000454.jpg; lacuna boundary canvases 438–439; shared final f. 219v canvas 454

notes: The 97 encoded LG containers are representation metrics only. Skeat states that the manuscript marks paragraphs with large initials and that he inserted slight spaces at those points; group sizes vary too widely to support 97 intrinsic stanzas.

Four additional L elements occur inside two editorial NOTE1 notes and contain conjectural supplied verse. They are editorial apparatus, not part of the direct poem stream or the 2,821-line intellectual-content count.
The collateral Cambridge fragment witness preserves eleven verse lines absent from Laud. Those readings are witness variants and are not silently inserted into this Skeat/U-M source representation.

extent.description: One verse romance in the immediate Skeat/U-M source representation. A raw XML count finds 2,827 L elements: 2,823 are direct children of the 97 LG containers, while four conjectural verse lines are nested inside two editorial NOTE1 notes. Of the 2,823 direct positions, two are asterisk-only display rows after conventional line 1444, leaving 2,821 extant verbal medieval verse lines. Conventional editorial numbering runs through 3001; 180 positions, lines 1445–1624, are absent because one manuscript leaf is lost. The final unnumbered direct L is conventional line 3001. These figures describe the Laud-based 1868/U-M representation, not a critical total across both witnesses; the Cambridge fragments preserve eleven additional lines absent from Laud. The carrier audit separately finds four DIV1 elements, 222 PB elements, 223 EPB elements, 97 LG elements, 135 NOTE1 elements, 63 manuscript MILESTONE elements, and eight P elements. The four DIV1 containers comprise one omitted-half-title placeholder, one omitted-front-matter placeholder, the poem, and one omitted-back-matter placeholder. The 222 PB/223 EPB scaffold spans the digitized 1868 carrier, while only the title page, poem, and inline notes are verbally encoded. The 97 LGs correspond to Skeat's editorial paragraph/display spaces rather than intrinsic stanzas, so sections remains null and verse_groups is retained only as an encoding-layer metric.

related_works.title: Lai d'Haveloc
notes: The Anglo-Norman lai is an earlier Havelok-tradition analogue. Madden printed it for comparison; Skeat emphasized that the French and English versions differ widely. No identified French manuscript is asserted as the English poet's physical exemplar.

related_works.title: Geffrei Gaimar's Havelok episode
notes: Gaimar's chronicle episode belongs to the wider Havelok story tradition and is closely related to the Anglo-Norman lai. It is retained as a conceptual analogue, not treated as an identified physical source for the Middle English poem.

related_works.title: King Horn
notes: King Horn follows Havelok in the same Laud hand and begins in f. 219va, the same column and canvas in which Havelok ends. Codex adjacency and shared copying context do not make it part of Havelok.

summaries.value: Parallel betrayals displace the child heirs of England and Denmark. Earl Goodrich imprisons the English princess Goldborou, while Earl Godard murders Havelok's sisters and orders the fisherman Grim to drown him. Grim recognizes Havelok's royal birthmark and miraculous light, escapes with him to Lincolnshire, and raises him at Grimsby. Havelok later labors in Lincoln, is forced into marriage with Goldborou, and learns through signs and dreams that both are royal heirs. Disguised as merchants, they return to Denmark, win Earl Ubbe's loyalty, defeat Godard, and restore Havelok's Danish kingship. Havelok then returns to England, defeats Goodrich, restores Goldborou, and rules with her after their coronation.

The repository source represents Skeat's 1868 title page, poem on printed pp. 1–86, and inline footnotes. It does not verbally encode his preface, separate notes section, glossary, index, or other omitted carrier matter, although page-break placeholders span the complete scan sequence. Madden's 1828 French lai, Gaimar text, introduction, and apparatus are related edition components rather than parts of the cataloged Middle English work.

Laud Misc. 108 is the sole substantially complete witness, not the only surviving witness. One leaf lost after f. 211 removes conventional lines 1445–1624. Three of the four physical scraps in the mid-fourteenth-century Cambridge fragment item preserve portions corresponding to lines 174–183, 341–364, and 537–549, including eleven lines absent from Laud. The fragments were published only in 1911 and therefore cannot be a source for either Madden's 1828 or Skeat's 1868 edition.

The romance includes child murder, attempted murder, imprisonment, warfare, mob violence, flaying, hanging, burning, and other punitive violence. These features are documented for responsible discovery and interpretation.

notes: Work composition region, Laud scribal dialect, Laud codex origin, and Cambridge fragment origin are kept as separate assertions.

The 1868 title page's historical unique-MS wording is retained as edition history but normalized in this record to sole substantially complete witness because current DIMEV and DMER records include the Cambridge fragments and the Laud text itself has a 180-line physical lacuna.

Rights and practical access for the medieval work, editions, electronic encoding, manuscript photographs, catalogs, and requestable Cambridge images are documented separately in the linked lineage manifest.

open_questions.question: How should the poem's precise composition date be delimited within the late-thirteenth- to early-fourteenth-century scholarly debate?
next_steps: Consult G. V. Smithers's 1987 critical edition directly and review the legal, historical, linguistic, and paleographical arguments surveyed by Thomas Liszka.
Retain the specialist repertory's broad end-of-thirteenth-century display until the underlying arguments justify a narrower normalized interval.

open_questions.question: Is a complete public digital facsimile of Cambridge University Library MS Add. 4407 (19), including all four scraps and eight surfaces, available under an identifier not found in the scoped search?
next_steps: Ask Cambridge University Library to confirm whether Add. 4407 (19) has an unpublished or differently cataloged digital surrogate.

If no public surrogate exists, request professional color images of the complete physical item—all four scraps, both sides, housing/envelope, edges and scale/color target where offered—not only the three Havelok-bearing scraps.
Record the exact supplied-image licence before any redistribution or publication.
notes: The dated negative finding covers searches of Cambridge Digital Library, Fragmentarium, the general web, and plausible CUDL IIIF identifiers. It is not a claim that the item has never been digitized.

evidence.citation: General Corpus pinned CME source, source/CME_phase_1-2/aha2626.xml at commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.locator: complete file; HEADER, TITLEPAGE, FRONT, BODY poem, inline NOTE1 elements, and BACK
evidence.summary: Exact evidence for AHA2626 identifiers, 2006 uncorrected OCR and TEI-in-Libraries Level 1 statements, the 1868 title page, direct poem text, printed pp. 1–86, conventional lineation, inline editorial notes, manuscript milestones, and omitted-matter boundaries.

evidence.citation: Target-scoped XML structure, ancestry, and lineation audit of the pinned aha2626 source.
evidence.locator: all HEADER, FRONT, BODY, BACK, DIV1, PB, EPB, LG, L, NOTE1, MILESTONE, and P elements
evidence.summary: Found four DIV1, 222 PB, 223 EPB, 97 LG, 2,827 L, 135 NOTE1, 63 MILESTONE, and eight P elements. Of the 2,827 L elements, 2,823 are direct LG children and four are conjectural verse nested in two editorial notes; two direct rows are asterisk-only lacuna displays, leaving 2,821 extant verbal lines. Conventional numbering reaches 3001 with 180 positions, lines 1445–1624, absent. LG-size variation and Skeat's paragraph statement show that 97 LGs are editorial display groups rather than intrinsic stanzas.

evidence.citation: Walter W. Skeat, ed., The Lay of Havelok the Dane, Early English Text Society, Extra Series 4 (London: N. Trübner & Co., 1868).
evidence.locator: title page; preface §§1–3 and §26; poem pp. 1–86 = PDF pages 69–154; notes pp. 87 onward
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for the immediate source edition, its direct twice-through manuscript checking, Madden's corrections and review, editorial typography, 180-line lost-leaf gap, manuscript paragraph markers, omission of the full French texts, and scoped inheritance of Madden's text, notes, glossary, and preface.

evidence.citation: Frederic Madden, ed., The Ancient English Romance of Havelok the Dane, accompanied by the French Text, with an Introduction, Notes, and a Glossary (London: W. Nicol, Shakespeare Press, for the Roxburghe Club, 1828).
evidence.locator: introduction pp. i–lvi, especially lii–lvi; English-poem section pp. 1–104, with poem text pp. 3–104 = PDF pages 69–170
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for the first edition, direct permission to transcribe Laud Misc. 108, its literal-transcript claim and stated normalizations, 1270–1290 historical date proposal, manuscript description, complete English-text boundary, and separately printed French lai, Gaimar episode, notes, and glossary.

evidence.citation: Walter W. Skeat, 'A New Havelok MS.,' The Modern Language Review 6, no. 4 (1911): 455–457.
evidence.summary: First publication and full transcription of the Cambridge scraps d, e, and f: corresponding to lines 174–183, 341–364, and 537–549, with ten additional lines after line 544 and one further additional line in the following passage. It establishes the fragment witness only in 1911 and therefore outside the 1828/1868 source chain.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Catalogue of Western Medieval Manuscripts, MS. Laud Misc. 108; cached TEI description.
evidence.locator: Havelok msItem at ff. 204ra–219va; King Horn begins f. 219va; quire 19, layout, hand C, language, date, and production-unit descriptions
evidence.summary: Current official evidence for the exact Havelok locus, Middle English with Latin rubric, west-Norfolk scribal dialect, shared King Horn boundary, missing quire-19 leaf after f. 211, two-column 45-line layout, hand C, codex date bounds 1290–1400, and the distinct but early-joined production unit containing Havelok and Horn.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian complete IIIF facsimile of Bodleian Library MS. Laud Misc. 108.
evidence.locator: canvases 423–454 / images/000423.jpg–images/000454.jpg / ff. 204r–219v; lacuna boundary canvases 438–439; shared end canvas 454
evidence.summary: Visual inspection confirms Havelok's rubric and opening on f. 204r, the physical lost-leaf boundary between f. 211v and f. 212r, the resumption after conventional line 1624, and Havelok's end followed by King Horn on the shared f. 219v surface.

notes: Version 2.0-alpha.1 is a prerelease; census claims are pinned to this exact release.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance, 'Havelok the Dane' and manuscript records for Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108 and Cambridge University Library MS Add. 4407 D (19).
evidence.locator: work date, place, form, IMEV, plot, manuscript census, and modern-edition list; exact Laud and Cambridge manuscript records
evidence.summary: Specialist repertory evidence for an end-of-thirteenth-century composition, Northeast Midlands localization, aa4 bb4 rhyming couplets, two witnesses, Laud's late-thirteenth-/early-fourteenth-century date and missing lines 1445–1624, and the mid-fourteenth-century Norfolk paper binding fragments.

evidence.citation: Kimberly K. Bell and Julie Nelson Couch, eds., The Texts and Contexts of Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108: The Shaping of English Vernacular Narrative (Leiden and Boston: Brill, 2011).
evidence.locator: introduction pp. 1–20; Thomas R. Liszka, pp. 31–50; Andrew Taylor, pp. 71–86, especially p. 81
evidence.summary: Modern scholarly synthesis for the sole-substantially-complete-witness qualification, history and uncertainty of composition and manuscript dating, Matter of England and hagiographical-context classifications, the Laud romance production unit, and the Cambridge fragments' Norfolk association.
